# nff-sdk-c

Device-side C library for the [nff](https://github.com/nff-io/nff) IoT fleet management system.

Add MQTT fleet management, signed OTA updates, and crash reporting to any ESP32 or ESP8266 firmware in three lines of code.

```c
#include <nff.h>
#include "credentials.h"   // generated by: nff provision new-device --id my-device

NFF_CONFIG(cfg, "my-device", "nff.yourdomain.com");

void setup() {
    // ... WiFi connect ...
    nff_init(&cfg);
    nff_connect();
}
void loop() {
    nff_loop();          // non-blocking, returns in microseconds when idle
    // your application code
}
```

---

## What it does

The SDK maintains a second MQTT connection to the nff broker running alongside your existing application. The connection is device-initiated (no port forwarding needed) and runs at low priority — in steady state it adds ~4 bytes/minute of bandwidth overhead.

| Feature | What happens |
|---|---|
| **Heartbeat** | Publishes a retained `online` presence message on connect and every 30 s. The broker holds a `Last Will` that fires automatically on disconnect. |
| **Commands** | Receives signed MQTT commands from the nff server. Signature is ECDSA-P256 — commands without a valid signature are silently dropped. |
| **OTA** | Downloads firmware over HTTPS directly from a CDN (S3/R2). Verifies SHA-256, writes to the inactive partition, and reboots. On the next boot publishes `committed` or `rolled_back`. |
| **Crash reporting** | On any panic/watchdog reboot, assembles a JSON crash report from NVS metadata and an RTC pre-crash log (last 32 lines), and publishes it retained so nff receives it even hours later. |
| **Diagnostics** | Responds to `diag` commands with free heap, uptime, WiFi RSSI, and CPU count. |

---

## Requirements

| Platform | Prerequisite |
|---|---|
| Arduino (ESP32) | [PubSubClient](https://github.com/knolleary/pubsubclient) library installed in Arduino Library Manager |
| Arduino (ESP8266) | PubSubClient library |
| ESP-IDF | No extra dependencies — uses `esp-mqtt`, NVS, `esp_https_ota`, mbedTLS |
| Host (unit tests) | CMake ≥ 3.16, GCC/Clang, optional OpenSSL |

---

## Installation

### Arduino Library Manager

This repository is the **single source of truth**. The Arduino IDE/CLI, however, needs a
*flat* library layout (every file under `src/`, and the ESP32 port carrying a `.cpp`
extension because it is C++). Don't hand-copy — regenerate it:

```bash
python tools/sync_arduino_lib.py          # writes <sketchbook>/libraries/nff from this repo
```

This flattens the tree, duplicates `nff.h` to the library root + `src/`, renames the ESP32
Arduino port `.c` → `.cpp`, and excludes the non-Arduino ports. The transform is idempotent.
Install the git hooks once so the library re-syncs automatically on every commit/merge:

```bash
pwsh tools/install-hooks.ps1              # post-commit + post-merge → run the generator
```

> A plain symlink does **not** work: Arduino would compile all four guarded ports and choke
> on C++ in a `.c` file. The generated copy is the supported model.

### ESP-IDF component

Place the repository under `components/nff/` in your project:

```
your-project/
├── components/
│   └── nff/          ← this repository
├── main/
│   └── main.c
└── CMakeLists.txt
```

Add `nff` to your component's `REQUIRES`:

```cmake
idf_component_register(SRCS "main.c" REQUIRES nff nvs_flash esp_wifi)
```

---

## Provisioning

Before flashing, generate a `credentials.h` for the device:

```bash
nff provision new-device --id my-device
```

This creates `credentials.h` containing DER-encoded byte arrays for:
- Per-device mTLS client certificate (CN = device ID)
- Per-device private key (P-256)
- nff CA certificate (pinned — device rejects any other CA)
- Fleet command-verify public key (shared across all devices)

> **Never commit `credentials.h`.** It contains the device private key. Add it to `.gitignore`.

Place `credentials.h` in the same folder as your sketch or `main.c`.

---

## Usage

### Arduino (ESP32 / ESP8266)

```c
#include <WiFi.h>
#include <nff.h>
#include "credentials.h"

NFF_CONFIG(cfg, "my-device", "nff.yourdomain.com");

void setup() {
    WiFi.begin("ssid", "password");
    while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) delay(500);

    nff_init(&cfg);
    nff_connect();
}

void loop() {
    nff_loop();
}
```

### ESP-IDF / FreeRTOS

```c
#include <nff.h>
#include "credentials.h"

static const nff_config_t cfg = NFF_CONFIG_INITIALIZER("my-device", "nff.yourdomain.com");

void app_main(void) {
    // ... nvs_flash_init(), wifi_connect() ...

    nff_init(&cfg);
    nff_connect();
    nff_start_task();   // pins nff to Core 0, returns immediately
    // application continues on Core 1
}
```

### Custom commands

```c
static void on_valve_cmd(const char *payload, char *resp, size_t resp_len, void *ctx) {
    bool open = strstr(payload, "\"open\":true") != NULL;
    set_valve(open);
    snprintf(resp, resp_len, "{\"type\":\"valve_ack\",\"open\":%s}",
             open ? "true" : "false");
}

// Register before nff_connect():
nff_register_command("valve", on_valve_cmd, NULL);
```

### Pre-crash logging

Lines written with `nff_log()` survive a software reset and are included in the crash report:

```c
void sensor_task(void) {
    nff_log("sensor_task: reading temp");
    float t = read_temp();
    nff_log("sensor_task: t=%.1f°C", t);
    // if a crash happens here, both lines appear in the report
}
```

---

## MQTT topics

```
nff/devices/{id}/cmd          ← nff server → device (commands)
nff/devices/{id}/response     ← device → nff server (replies)
nff/devices/{id}/heartbeat    ← device → broker (retained presence)
nff/devices/{id}/crash        ← device → broker (retained crash report)
```

---

## Built-in commands

| Action | Description |
|---|---|
| `ping` | Returns `{"type":"pong","id":"...","fw":"..."}` |
| `reboot` | Acknowledges and reboots the device |
| `ota` | Downloads and installs firmware from a URL; publishes `ota_result` on next boot |
| `diag` | Returns heap, uptime, RSSI, CPU count |

All commands must carry a valid ECDSA-P256 `cmd_sig` field. Unsigned commands are dropped.

---

## Configuration

The `nff_config_t` struct is populated by the `NFF_CONFIG` / `NFF_CONFIG_INITIALIZER` macros from `credentials.h`. The only fields you supply are `device_id` and `broker_host`.

Optional tuning via `#define` before including `nff.h` (or via Kconfig on ESP-IDF):

| Symbol | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `NFF_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_S` | `30` | Seconds between retained heartbeat publishes |
| `NFF_LOG_LINES` | `32` | RTC pre-crash log buffer depth |
| `NFF_NONCE_RING_SIZE` | `16` | Command replay-prevention ring size |
| `NFF_TIMESTAMP_WINDOW_S` | `300` | Command timestamp acceptance window (±5 min) |
| `NFF_MQTT_BUFFER_SIZE` | `1024` | MQTT RX/TX buffer. **Must exceed the largest command** — an OTA command is ~421 B and PubSubClient's 256 B default *silently drops* it. Bump to `2048` for very long pre-signed URLs. |

> **Timestamp window & SNTP:** the `NFF_TIMESTAMP_WINDOW_S` check is **skipped** until the
> device clock is set (the SDK treats `millis()/1000 < 1e9` as "not yet SNTP-synced"). If you
> enable SNTP, make sure the clock is correct before `nff_connect()` or commands will be
> rejected once it crosses the window. Replay protection via the nonce ring is always active.

---

## Platform notes

### ESP8266

- No dual-core AMP (`nff_start_task()` is a no-op — use `nff_loop()` instead)
- No coredump partition — crash metadata from NVS only
- No ECDSA-P256 verify by default; define `NFF_SKIP_CMD_SIG=1` to rely on mTLS channel auth alone
- TLS uses BearSSL (lighter than mbedTLS, fits in 160 KB SRAM)

### Zephyr (V2 — stub only)

The Zephyr port compiles clean and returns `NFF_ERR_NOT_SUPPORTED` from all functions. A full Zephyr implementation is planned for V2.

---

## Running the host unit tests

No hardware needed. Uses the POSIX port with an in-memory mock MQTT.

```bash
cmake -B build
cmake --build build
# Windows (MinGW):
build\tests\test_nonce_ring.exe
build\tests\test_cmd_dispatch.exe
build\tests\test_ota_rollback.exe
# Linux / macOS:
./build/tests/test_nonce_ring
./build/tests/test_cmd_dispatch
./build/tests/test_ota_rollback
```

Tests cover: nonce replay prevention, ring wraparound, command dispatch (ping/unknown/user/unsigned/diag), OTA committed/rolled_back NVS flags, and anti-downgrade enforcement.

---

## Repository layout

```
nff-sdk-c/
├── include/
│   ├── nff.h              ← public API (only file you include)
│   └── nff_port.h         ← platform abstraction contract
├── src/
│   ├── nff_core.c         ← lifecycle state machine, public API
│   ├── nff_mqtt.c         ← MQTT connection and reconnect loop
│   ├── nff_heartbeat.c    ← retained presence publish
│   ├── nff_cmd.c          ← command receive, verify, dispatch
│   ├── nff_security.c     ← ECDSA verify, nonce ring, timestamp check
│   ├── nff_ota.c          ← HTTPS OTA download, SHA-256, rollback
│   ├── nff_crash.c        ← panic hook, RTC log, boot crash report
│   ├── nff_diag.c         ← diagnostics command handler
│   └── port/
│       ├── nff_port_esp32_idf.c
│       ├── nff_port_esp32_arduino.c
│       ├── nff_port_esp8266_arduino.c
│       └── nff_port_posix.c
├── examples/
│   ├── arduino_basic/     ← minimal Arduino sketch
│   └── espidf_basic/      ← minimal ESP-IDF app
├── tools/
│   ├── gen_credentials.py ← called by `nff provision new-device`
│   ├── sync_arduino_lib.py← regenerate the flat Arduino library from this repo
│   ├── install-hooks.ps1  ← install the auto-sync git hooks
│   └── hooks/             ← post-commit / post-merge (run the generator)
├── tests/
│   ├── test_nonce_ring.c
│   ├── test_cmd_dispatch.c
│   └── test_ota_rollback.c
├── CMakeLists.txt         ← ESP-IDF component + host test build
├── library.properties     ← Arduino Library Manager manifest
└── Kconfig                ← ESP-IDF / Zephyr menuconfig symbols
```

---

## Security model

Commands are authenticated at two independent layers:

1. **mTLS** — the device presents a per-device client certificate. The nff broker rejects connections without a valid certificate signed by the nff CA. The device pins the CA and rejects any broker not signed by it.
2. **Command signature** — every command carries an ECDSA-P256 signature over the action, URL, nonce, and timestamp. The device verifies this against the fleet-wide command-verify public key embedded in `credentials.h`. Commands pass or fail independent of the TLS layer.

**Verified OTA path (current V1, ESP32 Arduino):** signed `ota` command → HTTPS streaming
download → SHA-256 verification of the downloaded image against the hash carried in the
*signed* command → `Update.end()` boot-partition switch → reboot → `ota_result`. The image's
integrity is therefore bound to the command signature (a forged hash needs the command-signing
key). Note the Arduino port currently downloads with `WiFiClientSecure::setInsecure()` — the
firmware **server's** TLS cert is not pinned, so transport authenticity for the download relies
on the SHA-256 + command signature, not on the HTTPS cert.

**Production hardening (V2):** the bootloader's Secure Boot V2 verifies the firmware binary
signature against a key burned into eFuses, so a fully compromised nff server still cannot run
malicious firmware without the HSM-held build key. This hardware layer is independent of the
software OTA path above.

See [IOT_BRIDGE.md](../IOT_BRIDGE.md) for the full threat model and defence-in-depth chain.
